content.text: A GenAI feature launch is not a destination — it is the start of a measurement and iteration cycle. Without a structured post-launch review process, teams ship features and move on, accumulating technical debt and missing early signals of quality regression. A post-launch review template standardizes what gets measured, when it gets reviewed, and who makes the decision to continue, iterate, or retire.

attrs.alt: Post-launch review timeline showing four gates: 24h pulse check, 7-day operational review, 30-day full review, and 90-day graduation decision

content.text: Why GenAI Launches Need Structured Reviews
GenAI systems behave differently from traditional software after launch:
Quality problems are distribution-level, not binary — a feature that works for 90% of queries can still cause significant harm through the 10% failure tail
User behavior changes the effective distribution over time — queries sent in week 4 differ from queries sent in week 1 as users discover the system's capabilities
Cost and quality interact in ways that are not visible until real traffic arrives — eval scores on benchmark datasets do not predict token consumption patterns
Safety regressions can emerge gradually as the system encounters edge-case combinations of user input and retrieved context
These properties mean that a 24-hour post-launch check is insufficient. The review must continue across a 90-day window with increasing depth at each gate.
The 24-hour pulse check is the rollback gate. Its purpose is narrow: confirm the system is stable enough to continue running, or pull the rollback trigger. It is not a quality review.
The pulse check is executed by the on-call SRE and the feature team engineer, asynchronously via a shared document. It does not require a synchronous meeting.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class PulseCheckResult:
    feature_id: str
    error_rate_pct: float
    p99_latency_ms: float
    p99_baseline_ms: float
    token_consumption_vs_forecast: float     # ratio (1.0 = on forecast)
    safety_incidents: int
    all_checks_passed: bool = True

    def evaluate(self) -> Literal["pass", "investigate", "rollback"]:
        if self.safety_incidents > 0:
            return "rollback"
        if self.error_rate_pct > 5.0:
            return "rollback"
        if self.p99_latency_ms > self.p99_baseline_ms * 2.0:
            return "rollback"
        if self.error_rate_pct > 1.0:
            return "investigate"
        if self.p99_latency_ms > self.p99_baseline_ms * 1.5:
            return "investigate"
        if self.token_consumption_vs_forecast > 3.0:
            return "investigate"
        return "pass"

result = PulseCheckResult(
    feature_id="genai-document-qa-v2",
    error_rate_pct=0.3,
    p99_latency_ms=1850,
    p99_baseline_ms=1400,
    token_consumption_vs_forecast=1.4,
    safety_incidents=0,
)
print(result.evaluate())   # "pass"

If any rollback-trigger threshold is crossed, the feature lead must be notified within 15 minutes of the pulse check completing. The decision to roll back belongs to the feature lead, not the on-call engineer. The on-call engineer pages; the feature lead decides.

By day 7, the system has processed enough real traffic to reveal operational patterns that were invisible at launch. The 7-day review is a 60-minute synchronous meeting with the engineering team, SRE lead, and PM.

# 7-Day Operational Review: [Feature Name]
**Review date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Feature launch date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Attendees:** Feature lead, SRE lead, PM, QA

## 1. Traffic and Adoption
- Total requests processed: [N]
- Unique users: [N]
- Geographic distribution (any unexpected regions?)
- Query type distribution vs. pre-launch assumptions

## 2. SLO Compliance
| SLO | Target | Actual | Compliant? |
|-----|--------|--------|------------|
| p99 latency | < 2s | 1.8s | ✅ |
| Error rate | < 0.5% | 0.3% | ✅ |
| Availability | 99.9% | 99.95% | ✅ |

## 3. Quality Signals
- Eval score on production sample vs. pre-launch eval: [delta]
- User feedback thumbs-down rate: [%]
- Support ticket volume and top categories: [N tickets, top 3 categories]
- Any CSAT themes emerging?

## 4. Cost vs. Forecast
- Actual token spend (7 days): $[N]
- Forecast for 7 days: $[N]
- Variance: [+/-N%]
- Extrapolated monthly cost: $[N]
- Action if > 120% of forecast: [owner + plan]

## 5. Incidents and On-Call Load
- Incidents: [N] (P0: [N], P1: [N])
- On-call pages: [N]
- Time spent on feature-related incidents: [N hours]
- Runbook gaps identified: [N — link to KB tickets]

## 6. Open Issues and Action Items
| Item | Owner | Due | Priority |
|------|-------|-----|----------|
| [item] | @eng | [date] | P1 |

## 7. Decision: Continue / Iterate / Escalate
- [ ] Continue as-is (all green)
- [ ] Continue with action items (specific concerns noted)
- [ ] Escalate to leadership (cost, quality, or safety concerns)
- [ ] Rollback discussion needed

The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'Decision', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  section is mandatory and must record the explicit outcome of the meeting — not an implicit consensus. The feature lead signs off on the decision in the shared document.
The 7-day review includes a production sample eval: a random sample of 200–500 real queries from the past 7 days, evaluated by the production eval suite.

The 30-day review is the canonical post-launch review — the one that determines the feature's trajectory. It is a 90-minute meeting with all stakeholders, followed by a written document that becomes the public record of the launch's outcome.

# Full Post-Launch Review: [Feature Name]
**Version:** 1.0
**Review date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Launch date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Feature lead:** [Name]
**PM:** [Name]

---

## Executive Summary (3–5 sentences)
[High-level outcome: did the launch achieve its goals?
What surprised us? What is the recommendation going forward?]

---

## 1. Goal Achievement
| Goal | Target | Actual | Met? |
|------|--------|--------|------|
| User adoption | 10k users in 30 days | 8.3k | ⚠️ |
| Task completion rate | > 70% | 74% | ✅ |
| Support ticket deflection | 20% reduction | 18% | ⚠️ |
| Monthly token cost | < $5k | $4.2k | ✅ |

## 2. Quality Metrics (30-day trend)
- Production eval score trend (week-over-week): [table or chart reference]
- User thumbs-down rate trend: [sparkline or %s]
- p99 latency trend: [stable / improving / degrading]
- CSAT score vs. pre-launch baseline: [delta]

## 3. Incidents and Reliability
- Total incidents: [N]
- SLO compliance (30-day): [%]
- Postmortem action items completed vs. total: [N/M]
- Unresolved P1/P2 items still open: [N — link to backlog]

## 4. Cost Analysis
- Actual 30-day token cost: $[N]
- Forecast: $[N]
- Annualized projection: $[N]
- Cost per successful task completion: $[N]
- Cost optimization opportunities identified: [list]

## 5. Lessons Learned
### What worked well
- [item]
### What we would do differently
- [item]
### Surprises (good and bad)
- [item]

## 6. Technical Debt Audit
- Prompt hacks / workarounds introduced during launch: [N — link to tickets]
- Missing observability: [list]
- Known eval coverage gaps: [list]
- Estimated engineering-days to address: [N]

## 7. Recommendation
- [ ] **Continue GA** — feature is stable, goals met, continue monitoring
- [ ] **Iterate before GA** — specific improvements required (list with owners)
- [ ] **Reduce scope** — scale back to subset of use cases
- [ ] **Deprecate** — goals not achievable, plan decommission

**Rationale:** [2–3 sentences explaining the recommendation]

## 8. Next Review
- 90-day graduation review scheduled for: [date]
- Owner: [name]
The recommendation at day 30 must be based on criteria agreed before launch. Post-hoc rationalization of a decision already made is the most common failure mode of post-launch reviews.
Specific query categories clearly failing (> 20% thumbs-down)
Eval score drop > 10% or safety issues
< 50% of adoption goal, no recovery path

The 90-day graduation review determines whether a feature moves from beta to generally available (GA), or whether it needs a different trajectory. It is a leadership-level decision meeting, not an engineering-level operations review.

graduation_criteria:
  quality:
    eval_score_stability: "< 5% variance over 90-day window"
    production_sample_score: ">= 4.0 / 5.0 on 30-day rolling eval"
    thumbs_down_rate: "< 6% over trailing 30 days"
    safety_incidents_90d: "0 confirmed incidents"

  reliability:
    slo_compliance_90d: ">= 99.5%"
    p0_incidents_90d: "0"
    p1_incidents_resolved: "100% within SLA"
    on_call_load_per_week: "< 2 hours attributed to this feature"

  cost:
    cost_per_task: "within 15% of pre-launch forecast"
    cost_trend: "stable or improving"
    no_uncapped_cost_vectors: true   # no query pattern that could cause unbounded spend

  operations:
    runbook_coverage: "all known failure modes documented"
    alert_coverage: "all SLOs have corresponding alerts"
    ownership: "clear team owns this feature post-graduation"
    documentation: "user-facing docs and internal KB complete"

  business:
    adoption_goal: ">= 80% of 90-day target"
    retention_rate: "WAU/DAU ratio stable or improving"
    nps_delta: ">= 0 vs. pre-launch"

Any criterion marked as blocking ( {'type': 'text', 'text': 'safety_incidents_90d', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  and  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'no_uncapped_cost_vectors', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  in particular) must be met before graduation can proceed, regardless of the other criteria.

# Graduation Decision Record: [Feature Name]

**Decision:** ✅ Graduated to GA | ⚠️ Extended beta | ❌ Deprecated
**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Decision makers:** [names and roles]

## Criteria met
- [x] Quality — eval score 4.2/5.0, thumbs-down 4.8%
- [x] Reliability — SLO 99.7%, 0 P0 incidents
- [x] Cost — $0.04 per task completion (forecast was $0.05)
- [x] Operations — 12/12 runbooks complete
- [ ] Business — adoption at 73% of goal ⚠️

## Rationale for decision despite partial miss
Adoption shortfall attributed to onboarding friction, not product quality.
A/B test (n=2,400) showed 31% higher adoption when the onboarding flow was
simplified. This will be addressed in Q3 sprint 2, not a blocker for GA.

## Post-graduation commitments
- Onboarding A/B test: complete by 2026-08-01 (@pm-name)
- Adoption gap monitoring: weekly dashboard review through Q3 (@pm-name)
- Next scheduled review: 2026-10-01 (quarterly)

The data collection phase of each review — pulling metrics, formatting tables, computing trends — should be automated. Engineers should arrive at the review meeting with numbers already assembled, not spending 30 minutes pulling dashboards.
Teams that automate this data collection report that their review meetings spend 80% of the time on decision-making rather than data gathering — which is the intended purpose of the meeting.
Post-launch review dates must be scheduled at launch, not after launch. If they are not on the calendar before the feature ships, they will not happen.
Day of launch, scheduled for T+7
Day of launch, scheduled for T+30
Day of launch, scheduled for T+90
Scheduling at launch also creates implicit accountability: if the feature lead is not available for the 30-day review date they agreed to, they need to find a substitute — not cancel the review.
The post-launch review process creates the feedback structure that turns a GenAI launch into a learning cycle rather than a one-time event:
— narrow rollback gate focused on stability, not quality
— first quality signals from real traffic, cost vs. forecast, incident load
— comprehensive outcome assessment with a written recommendation backed by pre-agreed criteria
— leadership-level decision with explicit criteria for GA promotion or alternative trajectory
— review meetings should make decisions, not assemble dashboards
— reviews that are not on the calendar before launch do not happen
